section.title: Faith and Works
section.sectionRationale: This is the storm center of the letter. Having shown that favoritism betrays real faith, James asks whether a faith that produces no deeds can save at all. He argues that faith without works is dead, illustrates with a hungry believer turned away with words, exposes a mere-assent faith no better than the demons', and proves from Abraham and Rahab that living faith is shown, or justified, by works. This is the movement that seems to collide with Paul, and it must be handled with care.
section.thesis: A faith that produces no works is dead and cannot save; genuine faith is living and active, proven and completed by deeds, so that a person is shown to be righteous by works and not by a bare profession alone.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the useless faith that only talks; faith without works is dead; the demons believe and shudder; and Abraham and Rahab justified by living faith.

verses.text: What good is it, my brothers, if someone claims to have {{pistis|faith}}, but has no {{erga|deeds}}? Can such faith save him?
Suppose a brother or sister is without clothes and daily food.
If one of you tells him, “Go in peace; stay warm and well fed,” but does not provide for his physical needs, what good is that?
So too, faith by itself, if it does not result in action, is {{nekra|dead}}.
But someone will say, “You have faith and I have deeds.” Show me your faith without deeds, and I will show you my faith by my deeds.
You believe that God is one. Good for you! Even the demons believe that, and shudder.
O foolish man, do you want evidence that faith without deeds is worthless?
Was not our father Abraham {{dikaioo|justified}} by what he did when he offered his son Isaac on the altar?
You see that his faith was working with his actions, and his faith was perfected by what he did.
And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, “Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness,” and he was called a friend of God.
As you can see, a man is justified by his deeds and not by faith alone.
In the same way, was not even Rahab the prostitute justified by her actions when she welcomed the spies and sent them off on another route?
As the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without deeds is dead.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, belief. James does not attack faith itself but a certain counterfeit: a bare claim to faith that produces nothing. True pistis is a living trust that unites with and works through deeds; the faith that merely asserts and never acts he calls dead.

erga.gloss: Works or deeds, actions that flow from faith. For James these are not merit that earns salvation but the necessary fruit and proof of a faith that is alive. Faith and works belong together as body and breath, and works complete or perfect what faith begins.

nekra.gloss: Dead, lifeless. James uses it twice of a faith that has no works, framing his whole argument: such faith is a corpse. As a body without the spirit is a dead body, so faith without deeds is not merely weak faith but no living faith at all.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare or show to be righteous. The verb can mean to be reckoned righteous before God, as in Paul, or to be shown and vindicated as righteous, as here in James of Abraham and Rahab. James uses it of faith demonstrated and proven by works, not of earning acceptance with God.

basic.context: James asks a blunt question: if someone says he has faith but it never shows up in how he lives, can that faith save him? To make it concrete, he pictures a fellow believer who is cold and hungry, and someone who says, be warm and well fed, but gives nothing. Empty words. So too, he says, faith with no deeds is dead. Even the demons believe God is one, and it does them no good; they only shudder. Then he points to Abraham, whose faith was proven when he obeyed God about Isaac, and to Rahab, whose faith showed itself when she protected the spies. Real faith always shows up in action.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the passage people set against Paul, who says we are justified by faith and not by works. The strongest orthodox answer is that James and Paul are fighting different battles and using the same words in different senses. Paul asks how a guilty sinner is first accepted by God, and answers by faith alone, apart from works of law. James asks what kind of faith is genuine and saving, and answers a faith that works. When Paul says justified he means declared righteous before God; when James says justified he means shown and proven to be righteous. When James condemns faith alone, he means a faith that is alone, that stands by itself with no deeds, a mere claim. Both agree that we are saved by faith, and both agree that real faith produces good works. There is no contradiction, only two sides of one truth.
basic.originalLanguages: The key words are pistis, faith, and erga, works or deeds. James calls a workless faith nekra, dead, a corpse. And he uses dikaioo, to justify, in the sense of showing or proving righteous: Abraham and Rahab were shown to be right with God by what they did. This is a different use from Paul's declared righteous before God, which is why the two writers do not collide.
basic.typology: Abraham offering Isaac foreshadows the Father offering His only Son; Abraham's tested, obedient faith is the pattern of trust that rests in God's provision. Rahab, a Gentile prostitute welcomed into the people of God, prefigures the gospel reaching the outsider and appears in the line of Christ. The living faith James describes is fulfilled in the perfect faithfulness of Jesus.
basic.christ: Abraham's near-offering of Isaac points straight to God who did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all. The righteousness credited to Abraham by faith is the same righteousness given to all who trust in Christ. And Christ Himself is the great worker of the Father's will, whose living, obedient faith and love are the model and the ground of ours.
basic.god: God credits righteousness to those who believe Him, as He did Abraham, and He calls such a person His friend. He is not deceived by empty profession; He looks for a faith that is alive and shows itself. The same God who justifies the ungodly by faith also transforms them so that faith bears fruit.
basic.crossReferences: Paul quotes the same verse, Abraham believed God, to teach justification by faith, Romans 4:1-5 and Galatians 3:6. Genesis 15:6 and Genesis 22 stand behind Abraham's story. Hebrews 11 lists both Abraham and Rahab as heroes of faith. And Jesus said we are known by our fruit, Matthew 7:16-20.
basic.oneStory: The Bible tells of a God who justifies sinners by faith and then makes that faith fruitful, from Abraham who believed and obeyed to Rahab who trusted and acted. James applies this to the church: a faith that never changes how you live is no living faith at all. This does not contradict the gospel of grace; it guards it, insisting that the faith which alone saves is never finally alone, but works itself out in love as it did in Christ, the true and faithful one.

context.literary: This is the theological heart of chapter 2 and, in a sense, of the letter, gathering the earlier calls to be doers and to keep the royal law into a direct argument about the nature of saving faith. It proceeds by illustration, verses 14 to 17, dialogue with an objector, verses 18 to 20, and scriptural proof from Abraham and Rahab, verses 21 to 26, bracketed by the refrain that faith without works is dead.
context.historical: James likely writes before or apart from the sharpest Pauline controversies, addressing a practical danger among his readers: a faith reduced to verbal profession and orthodox belief without a changed life. Some may have misused slogans about faith to excuse a loveless, inactive religion, the very error James dismantles with the case of the hungry brother.
context.canonical: The passage sits alongside Paul's teaching on justification and, rightly read, complements it. Together with Romans and Galatians it forms the New Testament's full account: justification is by faith apart from works of law, and the faith that justifies is a living faith that produces works. Hebrews 11 confirms the reading by praising the very deeds of Abraham and Rahab as acts of faith.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose and refute a dead, merely professed faith, to show that genuine saving faith necessarily produces works, and to prove from Scripture that Abraham and Rahab were vindicated as righteous by a faith that acted.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is both argumentative and prescriptive. It describes the difference between living and dead faith and argues its case; its prescriptive force is a summons to examine whether one's faith is real and to let true faith show itself in deeds of love. It does not prescribe works as the ground of acceptance but as the fruit and evidence of faith.

debates.question: Does James contradict Paul on justification by faith?

views.label: No; they use justify and faith in different senses
views.case: This is the strongest orthodox reading. Paul asks how a sinner is first accepted by God and answers by faith apart from works of law, using justify to mean declared righteous before God. James asks what kind of faith is genuine and answers a faith that works, using justify to mean shown or proven righteous, as a tree is known by its fruit. When James denies faith alone he means a faith that is alone, a bare claim with no deeds. Both affirm salvation by faith and both affirm that real faith works; they defend one gospel from opposite errors, legalism on one side and empty profession on the other.

views.label: They stress complementary truths for different dangers
views.case: Others frame it less as different definitions and more as different pastoral targets: Paul fights those who would add law-keeping to grace, James fights those who would subtract obedience from faith. Even Paul insists faith works through love, Galatians 5:6, and that we are created for good works, Ephesians 2:10; James simply presses that same point from the other side.

debates.bearing: On either framing there is no real contradiction. The Reformation summary holds: we are justified by faith alone, but the faith that justifies is never alone. James guards the gospel against the deadly idea that one can be saved by a faith that leaves the life unchanged.

debates.question: In what sense was Abraham justified by works, verse 21?

views.label: Shown and vindicated as righteous
views.case: Abraham was already counted righteous by faith in Genesis 15:6, decades before he offered Isaac in Genesis 22. So when James says he was justified by works, he means his faith was demonstrated, proven, and brought to its intended completion by his obedience. The offering of Isaac fulfilled and displayed the faith that had long since been credited to him.

views.label: Faith perfected and confirmed in obedience
views.case: Others stress verse 22, that his faith was working with his actions and was perfected by them; the deed did not create the righteousness but matured and confirmed the faith. The Scripture was fulfilled, that is, the earlier verdict of Genesis 15:6 was borne out in action.

debates.bearing: Both readings keep James in harmony with Genesis and with Paul: Abraham was reckoned righteous by faith, and that living faith was later vindicated and completed by the obedience it produced.

study.originalLanguages: The debate lives in a few Greek words. Pistis is faith, and erga are works or deeds; James pits a workless pistis against a living one and calls the former nekra, dead, a corpse without breath. The decisive word is dikaioo, to justify. In Paul it commonly means to declare a sinner righteous before God, received by faith apart from works of law. In James it carries its other proper sense, to show or prove righteous, to vindicate: Abraham and Rahab were justified by works in that their faith was publicly demonstrated and confirmed by what they did. And when James rejects being justified by faith alone in verse 24, the alone he denies is a faith standing by itself with no deeds, not the faith-alone of the gospel. Read this way, James and Paul use the same terms differently and teach one harmonious truth.

typology.fulfillment: A foreshadowing of the Father who did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, Romans 8:32; tested faith resting in God's provision.

typology.type: Rahab the prostitute welcomed into God's people
typology.fulfillment: The gospel reaching the outsider; Rahab enters the ancestry of Christ, Matthew 1:5, a trophy of faith that acted.
Fulfilled in the faithful obedience of Jesus, whose trust in the Father was proven in perfect action and at the cross.

study.christ: Abraham's offering of Isaac on the altar points to the Father who gave His only Son, and the ram provided in the thicket foreshadows the substitute God supplies in Christ. The righteousness credited to Abraham by faith is precisely the righteousness now given to everyone who believes in Jesus, who was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification. And the living, working faith James commends finds its perfect pattern in Christ Himself, the pioneer and perfecter of faith, whose trust in the Father was demonstrated in flawless obedience unto death.
study.god: God is the one who credits righteousness to those who simply believe Him, as He did with Abraham, and who honors such a believer by calling him His friend. He is not fooled by words; He sees whether faith is alive. The same God who freely justifies the ungodly by faith also gives His people a new heart, so that the faith He counts as righteousness inevitably bears the fruit of good works. Grace that saves is grace that changes.

crossReferences.note: Paul uses Abraham to teach justification by faith apart from works, the counterpart James assumes and complements.
Abraham believed God and it was credited as righteousness, quoted by both James and Paul, dated before the offering of Isaac.
In Christ what counts is faith working through love, Paul's own statement that real faith works.
Saved by grace through faith, not by works, yet created in Christ for good works, holding both truths together.
Both Abraham's offering of Isaac and Rahab's welcome of the spies are praised as acts of faith.

study.oneStory: Scripture tells of a God who justifies sinners by faith and then makes that faith fruitful, from Abraham who believed and was reckoned righteous, then proved it on Moriah, to Rahab who trusted the God of Israel and showed it by hiding the spies. James brings this to the church to kill a deadly counterfeit: a faith that talks but never acts, no better than the trembling belief of demons. Far from contradicting the gospel of grace, he guards it, teaching that the faith which alone justifies is never finally alone but works through love, just as it did supremely in Christ, the faithful Son, in whom our righteousness is secured and our good works are born.

quiz.q: What is James's main point about faith that produces no works?

options.text: Such faith is dead and cannot save; genuine faith shows itself in action.
options.note: Verses 17 and 26: workless faith is a corpse, not living, saving faith.

options.text: Faith by itself, without deeds, is useless like empty words to a hungry brother.
options.note: True to the illustration in verses 15 to 17, capturing the same truth.

options.text: Works earn our salvation apart from faith.
options.note: James makes works the fruit and proof of faith, not the earning of salvation.

options.text: Faith and works have nothing to do with each other.
options.note: The whole point is that living faith and works belong inseparably together.

quiz.q: How is James's teaching best reconciled with Paul's justification by faith?

options.text: They use justify and faith differently; we are justified by faith alone, but that faith is never alone.
options.note: Paul means declared righteous before God; James means shown righteous by a living faith.

options.text: Paul fights adding works to grace; James fights subtracting obedience from faith.
options.note: True to the different dangers each addresses, complementary not contradictory.

options.text: Paul and James simply disagree about how people are saved.
options.note: There is no real contradiction; both teach salvation by living faith.

options.text: James teaches that faith is unnecessary if you have works.
options.note: James never sets works against faith; he insists true faith produces works.

quiz.q: What does the example of the demons in verse 19 show?

options.text: Mere intellectual belief, even correct belief, is not saving faith.
options.note: Verse 19: the demons believe God is one, and only shudder.

options.text: Even demons hold orthodox beliefs, yet it does them no good.
options.note: True to the verse, illustrating belief without trust or fruit.

options.text: Believing the right doctrines guarantees salvation.
options.note: The demons prove that bare assent to truth is not enough.

options.text: Doctrine does not matter at all.
options.note: James affirms the truth that God is one; he denies that assent alone saves.

quiz.q: In what sense was Abraham justified by works when he offered Isaac?

options.text: His faith, already credited as righteousness, was shown and completed by his obedience.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23: Genesis 15:6 preceded and was fulfilled by the deed of Genesis 22.

options.text: His faith was working with his actions and perfected by what he did.
options.note: True to verse 22, describing faith matured and confirmed by obedience.

options.text: His obedience earned his acceptance with God from scratch.
options.note: He was already reckoned righteous by faith years earlier; the deed proved it.

options.text: His works replaced any need for faith.
options.note: His works flowed from and demonstrated his faith, not replaced it.

quiz.q: Why does James bring in Rahab the prostitute?

options.text: To show a second case where genuine faith proved itself by action.
options.note: Verse 25: she welcomed the spies, faith shown in a costly deed.

options.text: To show that even an outsider's faith is vindicated by what she does.
options.note: True, underscoring that living faith acts, whoever the believer is.

options.text: To prove that a person's past keeps them from being justified.
options.note: The opposite: her acting faith justified her despite her past.

options.text: To argue that good deeds alone, without faith, saved her.
options.note: Her deeds expressed a real faith in Israel's God; they were not faithless works.

questions: James pictures faith that says warm words to a hungry brother but gives nothing. Where might your faith be more talk than action right now?
How does it help to know that we are justified by faith alone, yet the faith that justifies is never alone but always works?
The demons believe correct things about God and only shudder. What is the difference between believing facts about God and truly trusting Him?
Abraham's faith was proven when he obeyed God about Isaac. Is there an area where God is calling you to prove your faith by costly obedience?
If someone watched your life this week, what deeds would show them your faith is alive? Which one is God nudging you to start?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads Even the demons believe that, and shudder, where the printed text uses an em dash before and shudder; per house style it is rendered with a comma, preserving the wording without a dash.
Worthless renders the same idea as dead; a footnote notes that some manuscripts (BYZ and TR) read dead, matching the refrain of verses 17 and 26.
Justified renders dikaioo, here in the sense of shown or proven righteous; the faith alone that James denies is a faith standing by itself with no works, not the gospel's justification by faith.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 2:14-26. The BSB prints an em dash in verse 19; per house style it is rendered as a comma, preserving the wording without a dash.
